text: This is very relevant, since it raises awareness of the different ways data can be represented and manipulated, drawing a connection between the artist-focussed authoring tool and the source code representation. It's empowering, similar to something like Processing, SVG, or (my favourite) Flash.

text:
Hi Xandor!
I am looking forward to learning more about your ideas regarding simplifying web development. Brother and I have been working on a project (<https://wasp-lang.dev/> - it is still very early) where we are looking to achieve very similar thing -> we would like to make web app development much simpler for developers first, and then possibly also for non-developers, while keeping up with the current ecosystem. We think way to do this might be by building a (declarative) domain specific language specialized for web app development.
Let me know if this seems interesting, and I hope we will anyway have some discussion on this topic!

Let me know if this seems interesting, and I hope we will anyway have some discussion on this topic!

text:
Hi Martin! I've actually already seen your project and subscribed a while ago :slightly_smiling_face:
I like that your project is taking a declarative approach, I think it's easier to understand (assuming that the DSL reifies the _right abstraction_) for programmers and regular people in general.
I also have a vision of building stuff lower down the stack that would initially be useful to programmers, but what I'll build on that foundation in the future would be useful to non-programmers.
My vision is to first define a subset of JS that is purely functional. I've been able to do this to a fair degree using ESLint + Flow, but it doesn't go far enough, there are some serious gaps in Flow, such as no higher-kinded types, trouble understanding auto-curried functions (TypeScript is even _less_ capable in this regard). I may have to write my own typechecker :sweat_smile:
Then I'd like to take a leaf out of Luna's book and provide a visual way of representing code that conforms to this subset.
On top of this basis I may build declarative tools that work at a higher level.
I hadn't considered using a DSL.
Would be happy to chat more

text:
Oh great, glad to hear you subscribed :slightly_smiling_face:!

Exactly, that is why we are focusing on declarative approach for now, because we think it can be really simple to use. However, as you mentioned, it is important to pick the right abstractions so that the resulting DSL is simple, powerful, but also flexible enough, and that is tricky, we are focusing on that a lot at the moment, examining popular web frameworks in order to figure out the right abstractions. Also, not everything can be reasonably made declarative, so we are trying to figure out which features are good to describe in declarative fashion and which are not, and how to approach those down the road.

Standalone DSL also comes with its own set of problems -> all the support (IDE) that language needs, integration with existing languages and solutions, ...

It seems we share the big vision, although we have different approaches - very exciting, I would be happy to share insights in the future!

I hope you don't mind me asking, what is the motivation for using a functional subset of JS? Is that to provide "safer"/simpler experience to developers? And/or is it to enable you to build the next step, the visual editor, on a good foundation that matches the visual way of representing the web app?
Also, what about solutions like Purescript? I am guessing you want to go with Javascript due to its popularity and how easy it is to use? That certainly makes sense, just wondering hm!

Btw. is there a way to follow your work, maybe somewhere to subscribe? If not I will just keep my eyes open here on FoC slack!

elements.text: Oh great, glad to hear you subscribed

!

However, as you mentioned, it is important to pick the right abstractions so that the resulting DSL is simple, powerful, but also flexible enough, and that is tricky, we are focusing on that a lot at the moment, examining popular web frameworks in order to figure out the right abstractions. Also, not everything can be reasonably made declarative, so we are trying to figure out which features are good to describe in declarative fashion and which are not, and how to approach those down the road.

Standalone DSL also comes with its own set of problems -> all the support (IDE) that language needs, integration with existing languages and solutions, ...

It seems we share the big vision, although we have different approaches - very exciting, I would be happy to share insights in the future!

I hope you don't mind me asking, what is the motivation for using a functional subset of JS? Is that to provide "safer"/simpler experience to developers? And/or is it to enable you to build the next step, the visual editor, on a good foundation that matches the visual way of representing the web app?
Also, what about solutions like Purescript? I am guessing you want to go with Javascript due to its popularity and how easy it is to use? That certainly makes sense, just wondering hm!

Btw. is there a way to follow your work, maybe somewhere to subscribe? If not I will just keep my eyes open here on FoC slack!

text:
> I hope you don't mind me asking, what is the motivation for using a functional subset of JS? Is that to provide "safer"/simpler experience to developers? And/or is it to enable you to build the next step, the visual editor, on a good foundation that matches the visual way of representing the web app?
It is for both of these reasons. Purely functional code is _very_ declarative, and thus is much better suited to a visual representation. I also want a safer/simpler experience for developers. The final vision I have is very ambitious, and may not be feasible for me to achieve. In working towards that vision, as I am building the pieces required for it, those pieces should be _independently useful_ for others. If I am not successful at any given point of my journey towards the final vision, what I have built up until that point should still be a net win for me in the work that I am doing at the time.

> Also, what about solutions like Purescript? I am guessing you want to go with Javascript due to its popularity and how easy it is to use? That certainly makes sense, just wondering hm!
I have considered compile-to-JS solutions, but I have issue with the community size, the ecosystem size, and the interop (they all promise 100% interop with JS on the box, but the reality is sometimes not so easy). Having said that, I'm likely to have the same challenges, there's more to interop than syntax, transpilation and module systems.
Mostly, though, the biggest concern I have is _inheritability_. I want a project that was created using my stack to be inheritable even to programmers that aren't necessarily familiar with FP. Although they may struggle to understand code they've inherited from me at first if they're not accustomed to FP, if that code is just a subset of JS, they can disable/remove the tooling that limits the code to a subset, and carry on with the project more easily using the paradigms that they are already familiar with.
I think this is one of the big issues with low-/no-code tools atm, they're walled gardens: there's literally _no code_, users are just configuring an instance of the vendor's platform. I want to build something that is genuinely inheritable, not just on paper, but in a very practical sense. (there are also many other problems with no-code that I hope to tackle).
It is for this reason that the entire stack needs to be open source, and its pieces should strive to be independently useful. I aim to build communities around each piece so that there will be some measure of support for those that _do_ inherit from me.
> Btw. is there a way to follow your work, maybe somewhere to subscribe? If not I will just keep my eyes open here on FoC slack!
Not yet. I've got some abstracts and notes written out (and the 1st stab at the ESLint + Flow config), but I haven't published anything.
I'll be taking paternity leave soon (:tada:), so I hope to put together something public-facing during that downtime.
I'll be sure to update here when that happens.

I'll be sure to update here when that happens.

text:
Congrats :tada:  :slightly_smiling_face:!

What is your reason for inheritability, do you think that will be the common case with the audience you are targeting?
I believe there is a reason why inheritability is not present in no-code / low-code tools -> because it is hard to achieve, and at the end for most of them it is not that important, due to the use case they are catering to. But of course, if they did have inheritability, they could target more / different use cases, since developers could use it for prototyping and similar.

With Wasp, we have been looking at two things: inheritability and extensibility.
Extensibility would actually be interop that you mentioned -> can we allow people to combine/use existing parts of JS ecosystem together with Wasp, intermingled with Wasp?
And inheritability -> can we allow people to "eject" the code made by Wasp, so they can continue with it? We are mentioning that on our landing page.
In our case, extensibility is a must, while we would really like to have inheritability because it removes the "lock-in" effect, but we are aware it might be hard to achieve it, and its importance will be smaller and smaller as Wasp becomes more and more mature, so we are still evaluating how much time does it make sense to invest into inheritability.

elements.text: Congrats

!

What is your reason for inheritability, do you think that will be the common case with the audience you are targeting?
I believe there is a reason why inheritability is not present in no-code / low-code tools -> because it is hard to achieve, and at the end for most of them it is not that important, due to the use case they are catering to. But of course, if they did have inheritability, they could target more / different use cases, since developers could use it for prototyping and similar.

With Wasp, we have been looking at two things: inheritability and extensibility.
Extensibility would actually be interop that you mentioned -> can we allow people to combine/use existing parts of JS ecosystem together with Wasp, intermingled with Wasp?
And inheritability -> can we allow people to "eject" the code made by Wasp, so they can continue with it? We are mentioning that on our landing page.
In our case, extensibility is a must, while we would really like to have inheritability because it removes the "lock-in" effect, but we are aware it might be hard to achieve it, and its importance will be smaller and smaller as Wasp becomes more and more mature, so we are still evaluating how much time does it make sense to invest into inheritability.

text:
This talk is the reason the community is named "Future of Coding" rather than "Future of Programming" — we didn't want to have a collision.

Bret is one of the most-cited figures here, and a handful of folks from CDG and Dynamicland are here too. You've come to the right place :)

text: Protocol discovery?

elements.text: Protocol discovery?

text:
My feeling is that he's suggesting more than just a discovery protocol. Once both machines are running a common protocol, even if that protocol is used to bootstrap more sophisticated protocols, I feel like we've moved beyond talking to aliens.

I don't know where to draw the line, though. Is it fair to assume both machines speak tcp? ip? I don't think Brett is suggesting computers should organically re-discover ethernet based on analog probing, but I do think he's suggesting more than just <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bonjour_(software)|bonjour>.

My favorite take on it, which I don't think is an indented reading, is that computers need to share a common protocol with their neighbors, but not necessarily their neighbors neighbors.

text:
Super super interesting.

Couple of design decisions don't make sense yet. Like, why is there both an inspector and the ability to split a definition across a row?

How do definitions take args? Just through the same row/stack? That means you can't define a function spanning cells that takes an arg?

The `!` operator is weird. Just default to execution everywhere, and use `{}` everywhere to defer execution.

Anyways. This is interesting enough to get me to break out my twitter.

text: Also, the cyberneticians used to have a diagram like this in the 50s. This is just the grid version of that: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Randolph_diagram>

attachments.title: Randolph diagram
attachments.text: A Randolph diagram (R-diagram) is a simple way to visualize logical expressions and combinations of sets. Randolph diagrams were created by mathematician John F. Randolph in 1965, while he was teaching at the University of Arkansas.

elements.text: Also, the cyberneticians used to have a diagram like this in the 50s. This is just the grid version of that:

text:
Here's a previous conversation on this question that is still accessible: <https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/CLYCGTCPL/p1579116473013100>

Here's a blunt view on lightning talks, in text comic format.

You think, "it'd be cool to see what everybody is working on."
You say, "let's all do talks on our projects.."
You think, "oh but I don't want to hear Kartik drone on about his lame-ass project for the umpteenth time."
You say, "..but just $TOO_FEW minutes per person."

Ok Kartik, if you're right, why does YC do demo day? They're pretty smart people, and their audience is smart people too.

Demo day is intended to drive a certain kind of action. The audience is people who've honed their pattern recognition for a very specific format of yes/no decision. The emphasis is on the final decision rather than the road taken to get to it. And the organizers are aiming for a feeling of scarcity to pressure investors into quick decisions. Nothing wrong with any of that, but none of it feels relevant to this group, where the goal is thoughtful appreciation rather than rapid action. (Right?)

I'm not sure what the answer is. Maybe we need something more like Science Fair? Put up your shingle for a predetermined hour, hack on your project if nobody shows up. If somebody shows up you give them your schtick. If it's someone who's already heard the schtick, maybe you answer their questions. Or maybe they don't have questions, they just wanna watch you hack. Or if your buddy shows up, you pair. Most importantly, anybody who doesn't care about your project knows what time to claim a prior engagement for.

text:
What I like about the 2x2 grid examples you've shown is that to me they look iconic, familiar, self-revealing, animatable, composable, minimal, nestable, stylish, modest, subtle, mathematical, architectural…

Randolph diagrams.. aren't even all of that, and then less so.

text:
That's a really interesting take. Your points resonate with me. To go from here, let me pick out a few things I like about the demo day idea (and I encourage others to do the same), so that we can try to design something that keeps the good parts.

Things that I like about the demo day idea:
• It's a singular event, which will garner more attention than a recurring thing
• It's presentations (prepared) rather than conversations (improvised)
• There's variety — maybe _you_ won't come away thinking of every project, but each project will probably come away with someone new thinking about it
• This feels like something distinct from <#C0120A3L30R|two-minute-week> and the podcast. If we just do 2 minute presentations with a hard cutoff, that's just a live broadcast of <#C0120A3L30R|two-minute-week>. If we just do 1 project per event, and make the events regularly occurring, that's just a live broadcast of the podcast.

text: The science fair idea is interesting. Depending on the number of people who sign up to attend, we could group audience members together so that they travel from stream to stream as a panel. I've done some science/arts festivals that take this format, and it's sort of fun. The panel members get a bit of a rapport going, and the presenters get steadily better at their schtick.

text:
I went to school for game development, but I don't actually work in the games industry now. It's just something I do off and on for fun. 

A few years back I tended to work mostly with Unity, but the parts or Unity I enjoyed seem to have stagnated, so I try to avoid it these days.

In particular, I'm interested in game networking and using the GPU for non-graphics simulation. 

As it pertains to this slack community, I find gpgpu programming particularly interesting. It's a space where there's a real need to move beyond C inspired programming languages that also has very hard performance requirements.

text:
one of the problems with no code (and frameworks too, though not as strongly) is that if a project is successful, it will outgrow the initial platform on which its built. In the case of a framework, you can do a rewrite that still re-uses a lot of the existing code. In the case of a no-code platform, you have to rewrite from scratch.
The problem is that success for a lot of projects doesn't necessarily mean that there is money for a team of professional developers to rewrite. Success might mean that a no-code app is heavily relied upon internally, but the company couldn't afford to rewrite it.
No-code tools generally solve the problem of writing the code. But that's often all they solve. Syntax is not the hard part of coding (after a few months). The hard part is keeping things well organised and adaptable.
When a regular codebase gets out of hand, the code can be analysed and refactored with the help of tools. When a no-code app gets out of hand, :man-shrugging: . (I predict a healthy industry of useless no-code consultants that will help companies "maintain" no-code apps whilst making things worse, in about 5-10 years' time. Just look at sharepoint.)

I'm even hoping to have back-and-forth. If a developer writes code that adheres to the FP JS subset, I can generate a visual representation of it (the visual rep is just a projection of the AST, much like e.g. Prettier just projects the AST).
This would change no-code from a binary proposition (either yes or no), to a spectrum.
Citizen devs can make most of the "boring" parts of the app, and call in a pro when they get stuck. The pro can use the interface she's used to (text). It won't be a choice between sticking with a no-code ball of mud, or an expensive complete rewrite by a team of devs. Each party can play to their strengths.

If its not inheritable, it represents a lot of risk to companies. Vendor lock-in risk, rewrite risk, etc. which makes it less appealing. Knowing you can hit eject (or not even eject, but add a pro dev to a citizen dev team), will be an attractive proposition, and as you said, can also be used as a prototyping tool.

text:
I suggest you work on reducing the amount of punctuation in your syntax. For example, the token 'break-if-= $term:loop' should probably be reduced to 'breakif'. Punctuation characters cause the user to slow massively down in their typing. Few people have memorized the punctuation characters. And it impairs readability massively when the key operator is the = sign afterwards. so 'breakif ='. but since it is so frequently used, how about 'bif ='.  Also the break statement having to specify the name of the block it is breaking out of is a huge waste of typing. 99% of the time you are breaking out of only one level, so make it the default unless you override it with an explicit label (as so many other languages do).

Also, in this day and age, having to refer to registers the same way intel does is counterproductive. In the 8086 you ad the AL and AH registers because people were doing byte based stuff. AX was the 16 bit register. then 32 bits came along and they called that EAX. Why not go in the reverse, where you assume 64 or 32 bit registers, call that R1, R2, ... RN., and if you want to specify 16-bit parts you use a longer string, like RL1, RH1 ,and if you want to just grab a single byte (very rare), you use RLL1. The point is to take the most frequent operations and make them short, and let the less frequent operations. Of course it goes without saying to avoid nutty instructions like the insane intel GF2P8AFFINEINVQB instruction, which to me is the most obvious sign of intel's decline.

At present your calculator sample is so many characters that i would consider it a punishment to use the language. You've got to be ruthless about abandoning historical notations, and arrive at one that is compact, readable, and avoids unnecessary use of hard to type characters.

I suggest you work on reducing the amount of punctuation in your syntax. For example, the token 'break-if-= $term:loop' should probably be reduced to 'breakif'. Punctuation characters cause the user to slow massively down in their typing. Few people have memorized the punctuation characters. And it impairs readability massively when the key operator is the = sign afterwards. so 'breakif ='. but since it is so frequently used, how about 'bif ='.  Also the break statement having to specify the name of the block it is breaking out of is a huge waste of typing. 99% of the time you are breaking out of only one level, so make it the default unless you override it with an explicit label (as so many other languages do).

Also, in this day and age, having to refer to registers the same way intel does is counterproductive. In the 8086 you ad the AL and AH registers because people were doing byte based stuff. AX was the 16 bit register. then 32 bits came along and they called that EAX. Why not go in the reverse, where you assume 64 or 32 bit registers, call that R1, R2, ... RN., and if you want to specify 16-bit parts you use a longer string, like RL1, RH1 ,and if you want to just grab a single byte (very rare), you use RLL1. The point is to take the most frequent operations and make them short, and let the less frequent operations. Of course it goes without saying to avoid nutty instructions like the insane intel GF2P8AFFINEINVQB instruction, which to me is the most obvious sign of intel's decline.

At present your calculator sample is so many characters that i would consider it a punishment to use the language. You've got to be ruthless about abandoning historical notations, and arrive at one that is compact, readable, and avoids unnecessary use of hard to type characters.

text:
Thanks for the feedback, Edward. My sense is that `break if` is easier to _read_ even if `bif` is easier to type. But that's very much a hypothesis. Certainly my hands would suffer less RSI if I switched over. I'd appreciate feedback from others.

> 99% of the time you are breaking out of only one level, so make it the default unless you override it with an explicit label.
Yup, that is already the case. Are you seeing the innermost level redundantly specified somewhere? Notice that there's an intervening '{' between `break-if-= $term:loop` and the `$term:loop` label definition (screenshot attached).

I would love to rename registers, but then a reader consulting the Intel manuals has to constantly translate in their minds. I consulted the Intel manuals intensely when I built the lower layers, so it's hard to be sure I've eliminated the need for consulting them by now. I try not to impose unnecessary labor on the reader. Labor I didn't have to perform feels particularly egregious.

There will likely be a RISC-V or ARM rethinking of Mu at some point, particularly in light of recent news. That will certainly fix the register names.

In general, I care a lot about minimizing how much a reader has to learn to start reading Mu code. It might sound funny to anyone who's actually tried to read Mu code, but it's very much true. I care more about the reading experience than minimizing characters; I think dropping letters has historically made code less approachable to non-programmers. As I said, I'll keep your feedback in mind, and I'll look for confirming feedback from others.

text:
Makes sense, I guess we could say there are two main benefits: 1. no lock-in effect, allowing platform to grow and 2. duality of the codebase, in the way that it can be worked upon by both devs and non-devs.

We are both achieving second point by restricting and organizing the code in such way that it allows us to build additional tools on top of it that utilize this structure to offer simpler interaction with the code (visual editor).
However, we are doing it via DSL that has interoop with JS, while you are doing it by restricting JS.

Well this is all so exciting, I wish I had more to share but we also don't know yet exactly how will DSL perform -> but you are on our subscription list so we will notify you in couple of months when we release the first MVP! Looking forward to seeing how your solution develops :slightly_smiling_face:.

text: <@UA14TGLTC> Yeah, that was basically what I was exploring in my screencast further down this channel (<https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/C5T9GPWFL/p1592379958360900>). But my working hypothesis is that my attempts to do so are misguided and almost certain to kill what makes spreadsheets special.

text: That was the first compiler tutorial I ever read, and it was awesome! That was my introduction to recursive decent parsing. Have you read "the dragon book", by chance?
